metric_value: The real interest by large IT organizations in the recent Oracle and Microsoft announcements is in Tier One support.
notes: Kastner as VP research director enterprise technology Aberdeen

metric_value: Enterprises don't want to be told that another vendor is responsible when things go wrong — the 'one throat to choke' approach — and want to know those calls will be answered by marquee names with enough money to provide high-end support. Oracle and Microsoft fit that role.
Microsoft and Novell announced interoperability and patent-covenant deal; Microsoft would resell SUSE Linux certificates and pledge not to sue Novell customers for Linux-related patents.
Oracle announced Oracle Unbreakable Linux — support for RHEL-compatible Linux at lower fees than Red Hat charges.
This new Microsoft-Novell collaboration agreement could allow significant virtualization of servers, reducing the number of physical platforms we'd have to maintain.
Kastner analysis implies Linux enterprise-support market will consolidate toward marquee vendors (IBM, Oracle, Microsoft-Novell) at expense of pure-play Red Hat.
Red Hat was acquired by IBM for $34B in 2019, becoming the flagship of IBM hybrid-cloud strategy; partial validation of Tier One consolidation thesis.
Novell acquired by Attachmate in 2011; SUSE Linux later spun off to EQT (2018) and returned to IPO (2021); Microsoft-Novell patent pact superseded.
